records.ety: [Icel. stakkr; akin to Sw. stack, Dan. stak. Cf. Stake.]

defs: A large and to some degree orderly pile of hay, grain, straw, or the like, usually of a nearly conical form, but sometimes rectangular or oblong, contracted at the top to a point or ridge, and sometimes covered with thatch.
quotes: But corn was housed, and beans were in the stack.

defs: An orderly pile of any type of object, indefinite in quantity; -- used especially of piles of wood. A stack is usually more orderly than a pile
quotes: Against every pillar was a stack of billets above a man's height.

defs: A pile of wood containing 108 cubic feet.
A large quantity; as, a stack of cash.
A number of flues embodied in one structure, rising above the roof.
Any single insulated and prominent structure, or upright pipe, which affords a conduit for smoke; as, the brick smokestack of a factory; the smokestack of a steam vessel.
A section of memory in a computer used for temporary storage of data, in which the last datum stored is the first retrieved.
A data structure within random-access memory used to simulate a hardware stack; as, a push-down stack.
The section of a library containing shelves which hold books less frequently requested.

records.ety: [Cf. Sw. stacka, Dan. stakke. See Stack, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stacked (stkt); p. pr. & vb. n. Stacking.]

defs: To lay in a conical or other pile; to make into a large pile; as, to stack hay, cornstalks, or grain; to stack or place wood.

To place in a vertical arrangement so that each item in a pile is resting on top of another item in the pile, except for the bottom item; as, to stack the papers neatly on the desk; to stack the bricks.
To select or arrange dishonestly so as to achieve an unfair advantage; as, to stack a deck of cards; to stack a jury with persons prejudiced against the defendant.
